food_subgroup: Roasted meat noodle dish (Siu Mei with noodles)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for roasted platter noodle specifically; 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for roasted meat noodle combination dishes; A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Roasted meat toppings are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles (around 150 g cooked) topped with three types of Cantonese roasted meats — typically roasted duck, char siu, and siu yuk — along with blanched bean sprouts and a soy-sesame sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Foodo (roasted duck, char siu, siu yuk, egg noodles) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (egg noodles cooked, bean sprouts); weighted composite per 100 g of assembled dish.
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Roasted Platter Noodle (Three-Combination)' as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighted-average component analysis using HPB Foodo entries for roasted duck (烧鸭), char siu (叉烧), and siu yuk (烧肉), combined with USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 172030) and mung bean sprouts. Approximate dish composition assumed: 37.5% egg noodles, 20% roasted duck, 20% char siu, 15% siu yuk, 7.5% bean sprouts/vegetables. Values represent a plausible central estimate; restaurant-level variation is expected.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed meat-noodle dishes; analogous estimate based on egg noodle GI (GI=47) from University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for mixed dish composition.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roasted Platter Noodle (Three-Combination). GI of 47 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (cooked), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The protein and fat content from roasted meats will further attenuate postprandial glucose response, making GI 47 a conservative upper-bound estimate for the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as: (47 × 74 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 34.8, rounded to 34.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2080 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 2080 mg sodium — nearly the entire WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and the seasoning of roasted meats; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced sauce, or avoid this dish on high-sodium days.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~35 per serving) from egg noodle carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat from roasted meats
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low GI (47) due to egg noodles, but the large carbohydrate load per serving (~74 g per 400 g) produces a moderate-to-high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, substitute with lower-carbohydrate noodles, or pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted pork belly (siu yuk) and char siu, combined with high sodium from soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (~2.6 g per 100 g, ~10.4 g per serving) from roasted pork meats and high sodium (~2080 mg per serving) represents a dual cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner roasted meat options such as roasted duck breast, and request sauce on the side.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Platter Noodle (三拼面, San Pin Mian) is a popular Singaporean-Cantonese noodle dish consisting of a bowl of cooked egg noodles topped with three types of Cantonese siu mei (烧味) roasted meats — most commonly roasted duck (烧鸭), char siu (叉烧, honey-glazed roasted pork), and siu yuk (烧肉, crispy roasted pork belly) — served with blanched bean sprouts, spring onion, and a seasoning sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sesame oil. The noodles are typically thin, springy egg noodles (wonton-style) that absorb the savoury sauce well. The dish is a staple of Cantonese roasted meat (siu mei) stalls found across Singapore's hawker centres and food courts. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly sweet from the char siu glaze, with a contrast of textures: tender duck, chewy pork, crackling pork belly skin, and springy noodles. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal (~195 kcal per 100 g, ~12.5 g protein per 100 g) with significant sodium content from soy-based sauces (~520 mg sodium per 100 g). The glycemic index is low (GI ~47) due to egg noodles, but the large serving size produces a moderate-to-high glycemic load. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension due to very high sodium per serving, and warrants portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as a whole. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted-average component analysis using HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy entries for the individual components (roasted duck, char siu, siu yuk, egg noodles, bean sprouts). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium because the component proportions are assumed based on typical hawker-centre serving conventions and may vary by restaurant.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated, not directly measured for this composite dish. Restaurant-level variation in meat ratios, sauce quantity, and noodle portion will affect actual nutrient content. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy component sources used. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g based on minor contributions from roasted pork fat; this is a trace estimate. 6. GI of 47 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodles (cooked) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle; the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat attenuation. 7. GL per serving = (47 × 74) / 100 = 34.78, rounded to 34.7; carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.5 × 4 = 74 g. 8. The dish is served at Hennessy Duck - Funan (field survey data, GD002942). 9. Halal status is not confirmed; roasted pork components (char siu, siu yuk) make this dish non-halal by default. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from component sources and carry moderate uncertainty.

Adopted GI=47 from University of Sydney GI Database (egg noodles, analogous estimate) with full documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
